core_behavior: The system must evaluate a candidate email address string against three mandatory structural rules: exactly one '@' symbol, non-empty local part, and domain containing at least one '.' character.
An address is valid if and only if all three rules are satisfied simultaneously.
Failure of any single rule causes the address to be rejected.
The string must contain exactly one '@' character.
The substring to the left of the '@' must contain at least one character.
The substring to the right of the '@' must contain at least one '.' character.
The validator does not verify deliverability, DNS resolution, or mailbox existence.
Split the input string on the '@' character; the resulting array must have exactly two elements.
If the array has one element, there is no '@' → reject (MISSING_AT).
If the array has three or more elements, there are multiple '@' symbols → reject (MULTIPLE_AT).
The local part is defined as the substring strictly to the left of the single '@' character and must have a length of ≥ 1 character after no trimming is applied.
A local part of zero length (i.e., the '@' is the first character) → reject (EMPTY_LOCAL).
The domain part is defined as the substring strictly to the right of the single '@' character and must contain at least one '.' character anywhere within it.
An empty domain (i.e., '@' is the last character) → reject (INVALID_DOMAIN).
A domain with no '.' character → reject (INVALID_DOMAIN).
Optionally, the system may also return a reason code identifying the first rule violated.

Reason codes are evaluated and reported in the order listed: NULL_INPUT, EMPTY_STRING, LENGTH_EXCEEDED, MISSING_AT, MULTIPLE_AT, EMPTY_LOCAL, INVALID_DOMAIN. Only the first applicable reason code is returned.
inputs: Source: Any caller: form field, API request, function argument, etc.
Encoding: UTF-8; no binary or null-byte inputs are accepted
Length accepted range: 1–254 characters (inclusive). Strings outside this range are rejected.
Null or undefined input is treated as invalid input; rejected with a distinct error signal.
outputs: The validator produces a binary result for each input: VALID or INVALID.
VALID means the address satisfies all three structural rules.
INVALID means the address fails one or more structural rules, or is malformed input.
Optionally (implementation-dependent), the system may also return a reason code identifying the first rule violated.
Reason code NULL_INPUT: Input is null, undefined, or non-string.
Reason code EMPTY_STRING: Input is an empty string.
Reason code LENGTH_EXCEEDED: Input exceeds 254 characters.
Reason code MISSING_AT: No '@' symbol found.
Reason code MULTIPLE_AT: More than one '@' symbol found.
Reason code EMPTY_LOCAL: Local part (left of '@') is empty.
Reason code INVALID_DOMAIN: Domain part (right of '@') contains no '.' character.
assumptions: No whitespace trimming: The validator does not strip leading or trailing whitespace from the input or from the local/domain parts. ' user@example.com' (with a leading space) is treated as having a local part of ' user', which is non-empty.
Whitespace is significant and is not stripped.
failure_modes: Null or undefined input is rejected with a distinct error signal.
An empty string input is rejected.
Input with no '@' symbol is rejected.
Input with more than one '@' symbol is rejected.
Input where the local part (left of '@') is empty is rejected.
Input where the domain part (right of '@') contains no '.' character is rejected.
edge_cases: A string where '@' is the first character results in an empty local part → reject (EMPTY_LOCAL).
A string where '@' is the last character results in an empty domain that satisfies neither the domain presence nor the dot requirement → reject (INVALID_DOMAIN).
Input with a leading space such as ' user@example.com' is treated as having a local part of ' user', which is non-empty.
Strings of length 1 to 254 characters are within the accepted range; strings outside this range are rejected.
